name: Single-loaded corridor layout
description: A circulation spine pressed against one outer wall, with a single row of rooms attached on the opposite side so every room opens to the outside on its long facade. Because the corridor itself takes the wall-facing position, rooms enjoy daylight from two sides — the long exterior wall and a short end wall — which is impossible in a double-loaded plan. The corridor is typically 2 blocks wide; rooms occupy the full remaining depth. This arrangement underlies monastic cloisters opening onto a garth, Mediterranean villa galleries facing the sea, motel room rows opening to a balcony walkway, and renaissance loggias. The corridor wall is often pierced by arched openings or windows rather than being solid, reinforcing the light-on-two-sides advantage.
placement_rules: Place the corridor against the outer wall whose exposure is least desirable (cold-side, street-side, or shaded-side) so rooms claim the better-lit opposite facade.
Keep corridor width at 2 blocks; the single-loaded layout is already wider than a double-loaded one and should not waste further footprint on circulation.
Punch frequent windows or arched openings into the corridor's outer wall — it is the public side and benefits from a colonnade or loggia treatment.
Each room's outer wall (opposite the corridor) carries the primary windows and earns most of the daylight; do not block this facade with stairs or service shafts.
Terminate the corridor at both short ends with a window, door, or stair so it never reads as a dead-end tunnel.
Doors from corridor to rooms space evenly along the inner wall; align them with corridor arches when present to create cross-views from outside to room interior.
lighting_ventilation: Light arrives on two sides of each room: the long outer facade (full windows) and a short end wall where geometry permits. The corridor itself is daylit through its outer-wall openings, so artificial lighting is only needed at night — place lanterns every 4-5 blocks along the room-side wall.

mediterranean.notes: Whitewashed plaster with sandstone arches along the corridor's outer wall; terracotta or smooth-sandstone floor; corridor reads as a shaded loggia overlooking a courtyard or garden.

rustic.notes: Farmhouse gallery: cobble base under a timber-framed corridor open to the yard via wooden posts and lintels; rooms behind keep stone walls for warmth.

renaissance.notes: Loggia of regularly spaced quartz pilasters supporting a coffered ceiling; corridor opens to a formal garden through arcades, rooms on the inner side enjoy framed garden views.
